message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 16
Recent Green phase: Implemented the policy-wide loyalty discount. Added LOYALTY_DISCOUNT=0.2 and LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS=2. Extracted componentGroupBases(items) returning per-component-group base premiums (block pricing, no surcharge), now reused by: policyBasePremium(items) (sum of unmodified item base premiums — the basis for policy-wide modifiers) and itemsPremium(items) (surcharged premium = main-item surcharged premiums + component group bases with first-insurance applied). Added loyaltyDiscount(yearsWithMHPCO, policyBase) returning 0.2*policyBase when years>=2 else 0. quotePremium(items, yearsWithMHPCO) now = roundPremiumUp(itemsPremium − loyaltyDiscount + fee). runScenario threads customer.yearsWithMHPCO into quotePremium. Removed the now-unused componentGroupPremium.

Refactor while keeping all 16 tests green. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, then APP mass. 

IMPORTANT context for upcoming tests (do NOT implement them, but keep the design open): follow-up contract discount (−15% of policy base, applies to each contract after the customer's first quote in the scenario) is policy-wide like loyalty — note that quotePremium currently has no notion of "which contract number" this is; that state lives in runScenario (sequential steps). Consider whether the policy-wide-discount structure (loyaltyDiscount) generalizes cleanly, but do not build the follow-up feature yet. Keep all tests green.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 16 tests pass. Now let me analyze the code for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Let me evaluate each name against the latest changes:

- `componentGroupBases` — clear, returns per-component-group base premiums. Good.
- `policyBasePremium` — sum of unmodified item bases; basis for policy-wide modifiers. Good.
- `itemsPremium` — surcharged premium. Good.
- `loyaltyDiscount(yearsWithMHPCO, policyBase)` — returns the discount amount. Reasonable.
- `quotePremium` — final rounded premium. Good.

The names are accurate. Let me look at the structure more carefully, especially given the upcoming follow-up discount feature (policy-wide, like loyalty).

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (focus area)

The most notable observation: `loyaltyDiscount` returns a **discount amount** (a positive number that gets subtracted). The upcoming follow-up discount is also policy-wide and also a percentage of `policyBase`. The hint asks me to consider whether `loyaltyDiscount` generalizes cleanly.

Let me examine the `quotePremium` structure and the duplication between `policyBasePremium` and `itemsPremium`.

**Current `quotePremium` (lines 106-110):**
```
const surcharged = itemsPremium(items);              // bindings:2, invocation:1
const discount = loyaltyDiscount(yearsWithMHPCO, policyBasePremium(items)); // bindings:3, invocations:2
return roundPremiumUp(surcharged - discount + PROCESSING_FEE);  // invocations:1(round) + 2 sub/add(2*2=4)... bindings: surcharged,discount,PROCESSING_FEE
```

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) + generalization for upcoming feature

The key insight from the hint: the follow-up discount is **policy-wide like loyalty**, computed as a rate × `policyBase`. Currently `loyaltyDiscount` bakes both the *condition* (years ≥ 2) and the *rate* (0.2) into one function. 

The cleanest generalization that **reveals intent** and keeps the design open: extract the notion of a "policy-wide discount rate applied to the policy base." A policy-wide discount is fundamentally `rate × policyBase` where `rate` is conditional.

Let me introduce a `policyWideDiscount(rate, policyBase)` helper expressing the common structure, and keep `loyaltyDiscount` defined in terms of it. This makes the upcoming follow-up discount a natural sibling without building it now.

Let me make this single, focused refactoring:

content.text:
All 16 tests still green. Let me reconsider whether this refactoring genuinely improves intent and APP, or whether it adds an element prematurely.

## Step 5: Re-evaluate the refactoring

The change separated the **rate decision** (`loyaltyDiscountRate`) from the **discount computation** (`policyWideDiscount = rate × policyBase`). This:

- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Improved. "A policy-wide discount is a rate times the policy base" is now explicit and named, separating *whether/how much* (the rate, which is loyalty-specific and conditional) from *how it's applied* (uniform across all policy-wide discounts).
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: This factors out the `rate × policyBase` structure that the upcoming follow-up discount will share, avoiding future conceptual duplication. It keeps the design open exactly as the hint requested, without building the feature.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: It adds one element (`policyWideDiscount`) and renames one. This is justified by the clear separation of concerns and the imminent second consumer.

**APP Mass comparison (the affected region):**

*Before* — `loyaltyDiscount` + `quotePremium` discount line:
- `loyaltyDiscount`: bindings (yearsWithMHPCO, policyBase) = 2; conditional 1 = 4; constants/bindings (LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS, LOYALTY_DISCOUNT, policyBase) = 3; invocation (×) — multiply is an operation = 2. ≈ 11
- `quotePremium` discount: invocations (loyaltyDiscount, policyBasePremium) = 4; bindings = 2. ≈ 6
- Subtotal ≈ 17

*After* — `policyWideDiscount` + `loyaltyDiscountRate` + `quotePremium` discount line:
- `policyWideDiscount`: bindings (rate, policyBase) = 2; invocation (×) = 2. ≈ 4
- `loyaltyDiscountRate`: binding (yearsWithMHPCO) = 1; conditional 1 = 4; bindings (LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS, LOYALTY_DISCOUNT) = 2. ≈ 7
- `quotePremium` discount: invocations (policyWideDiscount, loyaltyDiscountRate, policyBasePremium) = 6; bindings = 2. ≈ 8
- Subtotal ≈ 19

Mass rose slightly (≈17 → ≈19) due to one extra invocation in `quotePremium`. Per the rules, **Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP**, and the small mass increase buys a clean seam for the upcoming policy-wide follow-up discount.

However, I want to double-check this isn't *over-engineering* (Rule 4 violation) for a feature not yet built. The hint explicitly says "Consider whether the policy-wide-discount structure generalizes cleanly, but do not build the follow-up feature yet. Keep the design open." This refactoring does exactly that: it names the shared structure without building the feature. The separation is also independently justified — a discount *rate* and the *application of a rate to a base* are genuinely distinct concepts, and the conditional-rate pattern already exists for surcharges (`surchargeWhen`).

Actually, let me check for a symmetry opportunity: surcharges use `surchargeWhen(applies, rate)` returning a conditional rate. The loyalty rate is the discount-side analogue. Let me confirm `surchargeWhen` could express `loyaltyDiscountRate` for consistency:

content.text: There's a `surchargeWhen(applies, rate)` helper that returns `applies ? rate : 0` — exactly the conditional-rate shape. `loyaltyDiscountRate` is the discount-side mirror: `years >= threshold ? RATE : 0`. I can express it with the existing `surchargeWhen` helper, removing the duplicated conditional structure (Rule 3) and reusing an existing element (Rule 4). The helper name `surchargeWhen` is now slightly narrow, but it's purely "rate when condition" — let me generalize its name to `rateWhen` since it now serves both surcharges and discounts. This keeps intent honest (Rule 2).

content.text: All 16 tests green. Let me read the final state to confirm coherence.

The refactored code is coherent and all tests pass.

## Refactoring Complete

**File:** `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_04-34-58_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

### Step 1 — Naming Evaluation (first priority)
Reviewed all names against the latest Green phase. Found one name whose scope had grown stale: `surchargeWhen(applies, rate)` was named for surcharges only, but its shape — "this rate when the condition holds, else 0" — applies equally to policy-wide discounts. Renamed to `rateWhen` so it honestly reveals its now-shared intent. All other names (`componentGroupBases`, `policyBasePremium`, `itemsPremium`, `quotePremium`) accurately describe their behavior and were kept.

### Refactorings applied (one change at a time, tests green after each)

1. **Split the discount concept (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent).** Replaced `loyaltyDiscount(years, base)` — which conflated *the rate decision* with *applying the rate to the base* — with two named concepts: `policyWideDiscount(rate, policyBase) = rate * policyBase` (uniform across all policy-wide discounts) and `loyaltyDiscountRate(years)` (the loyalty-specific conditional rate). This makes "a policy-wide discount is a rate times the policy base" explicit and creates a clean seam for the upcoming follow-up-contract discount (also rate × policyBase) without building it.

2. **Removed conceptual duplication (Rule 3) and reused an existing element (Rule 4).** `loyaltyDiscountRate` was `years >= threshold ? RATE : 0` — the exact conditional-rate shape already captured by `surchargeWhen`. Generalized that helper to `rateWhen` and expressed `loyaltyDiscountRate` in terms of it, so the discount and surcharge sides now share one conditional-rate primitive.

### APP mass
The discount region rose slightly (≈17 → ≈19, one extra invocation in `quotePremium`). Per the rules, **Rule 2 trumps APP**: the small increase buys explicit intent and a shared seam for the imminent second policy-wide discount. Reusing `rateWhen` (Refactoring 2) recovered part of that by eliminating a duplicated conditional.

### Design left open for upcoming tests
The follow-up-contract discount (−15% of policy base, applied to contracts after the first in a scenario) now drops in as a sibling rate fed into the same `policyWideDiscount`. The "which contract number" state still correctly lives in `runScenario` (sequential steps); `quotePremium` will need a parameter for that follow-up rate when the feature is built — no premature plumbing added now.

### Tests
All 16 passing, 22 todo. No tests modified.
